key_insight: The paper characterizes when multi-snapshot spike deconvolution becomes locally well-behaved after variable projection, linking the convexity basin to PSF bandwidth, smoothness, and spike separation.
engineering_relevance: It gives practical guidance for initializing and refining super-resolution estimators so they can recover sparse sources reliably from noisy multi-snapshot measurements.
method: Eliminate amplitudes analytically to form a nonconvex least-squares problem over spike locations only (VarProSD).
Derive explicit basin-of-convexity conditions using PSF power spectral density and smoothness assumptions.
Use Beurling--Selberg extremal approximations to obtain sharp conditioning bounds and prove local descent/convergence guarantees.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments validating the theory and showing modified ESPRIT initialization followed by gradient-based refinement works well.
watch_item: The guarantees are local, so performance depends on starting inside the basin of convexity and on PSF/separation conditions that may be restrictive in practice.
paper_title: Characterization of the basin of convexity for multi-snapshot spike deconvolution via variable projection
